perturbation.original_text: Recipient may disclose the Product only to its employees who have a need to know in order to accomplish the purposes identified in Section l(a), and such employees' use of the Product shall take place solely at Recipient's site. Recipient will have executed appropriate written agreements with its employees sufficient to enable it to comply with the terms of this Agreement.
perturbation.changed_text: Recipient may disclose the Product only to its employees, and such employees' use of the Product can take place at any location. Recipient is not required to execute any written agreements with its employees to comply with this agreement.
perturbation.explanation: Removing the 'need to know' and 'site only' restrictions, as well as removing the requirement for written agreements, contradicts standard confidentiality and trade secret protections.
perturbation.law_explanation: The modified text weakens the protections a company takes to safeguard its trade secrets by permitting disclosure without need-to-know restrictions and without employee agreements. This makes it harder for Microsoft to argue that it took reasonable measures to protect its trade secrets, as required to claim damages or injunctive relief under the DTSA.

perturbation.justification: The modified text directly contradicts the reasonable measures a company must take to protect trade secrets, as suggested by the LLM's explanation and the definition of the 'Misaligned Terminology - Legal Contradiction' perturbation type. The original text included restrictions and safeguards (need-to-know, on-site use, and written agreements) that were removed in the modified text. Snippet 1 references the Defend Trade Secrets Act, which implies a standard for protecting trade secrets. Removing these protections would make it harder to argue that reasonable measures were taken. Thus, the removal of restrictions and requirements for written agreements directly undermines the legal intent to protect trade secrets, creating a clear contradiction.

perturbation.original_text: This Agreement shall be governed by the laws of the State of Washington and Recipient further consents to jurisdiction by the state and federal courts sitting in the State of Washington.
perturbation.changed_text: This Agreement shall be governed by principles of equity and fairness, irrespective of the laws of any particular jurisdiction. The Recipient consents to resolving disputes through binding arbitration, which may not be subject to judicial review.
perturbation.explanation: Changing the governing law to 'principles of equity and fairness' and mandating arbitration without judicial review circumvents established legal frameworks and statutory rights.
perturbation.law_explanation: While the FAA supports arbitration, it does so within a framework of established contract law and judicial oversight. Removing the applicability of state law and eliminating judicial review undermines the FAA's structure, which expects arbitration agreements to be enforceable 'save upon such grounds as exist at law or in equity for the revocation of any contract'.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_1: 9 U.S. Code § 1 - “Maritime transactions” and “commerce” defined; exceptions to operation of title | U.S. Code | US Law | LII / Legal Information Institute Please help us improve our site! × No thank you Quick search by citation: Title Section Go! 9 U.S. Code § 1 - “Maritime transactions” and “commerce” defined; exceptions to operation of title U.S. Code Notes prev | next “ Maritime transactions ”, as herein defined, means charter parties, bills of lading of water carriers, agreements relating to wharfage, supplies furnished vessels or repairs to vessels, collisions, or any other matters in foreign commerce which, if the subject of controversy, would be embraced within admiralty jurisdiction; “commerce” , as herein defined, means commerce among the several States or with foreign nations, or in any Territory of the United States or in the District of Columbia, or between any such Territory and another, or between any such Territory and any State or foreign nation, or between the District of Columbia and any State or Territory or foreign nation, but nothing herein contained shall apply to contracts of employment of seamen, railroad employees, or any other class of workers engaged in foreign or interstate commerce. CHAPTER 1—GENERAL PROVISIONS Sec. 1. “Mari
perturbation.justification: The original text specifies that the agreement is governed by the laws of Washington, while the changed text replaces this with 'principles of equity and fairness, irrespective of the laws of any particular jurisdiction' and mandates arbitration without judicial review. This creates a contradiction because it attempts to bypass established legal frameworks. While the scraped snippets don't directly address this circumvention, the Law Explanation explicitly states that this undermines the FAA's structure, which expects arbitration agreements to be enforceable 'save upon such grounds as exist at law or in equity for the revocation of any contract'. The changed text eliminates the applicability of state law, which the original agreement was subject to.

perturbation.original_text: The Product and related information is proprietary and confidential information to MS and its suppliers. Recipient agrees not to disclose or provide the Product, documentation, or any related information (including the Product features or the results of use or testing) to any third party or use the Product for any purpose other than as provided in this Agreement.
perturbation.changed_text: The Product and related information is proprietary information to MS and its suppliers. Recipient may disclose the Product, documentation, or any related information to any third party without the need for consent if the recipient feels that it is in the public interest.
perturbation.explanation: The original text protects the confidentiality of the product. This changed text allows the recipient to share the information if they feel it is in the 'public interest', directly contradicting the confidentiality agreement.
perturbation.law_explanation: The Economic Espionage Act makes it a federal crime to misappropriate trade secrets, especially when done with the intent to benefit a foreign power or with knowledge that the offense will injure the owner of the trade secret. By allowing the Recipient to disclose proprietary information under a vague 'public interest' standard, the modified text potentially undermines Microsoft's ability to protect its trade secrets and could create circumstances that violate the Act if the disclosure harms Microsoft and benefits a competitor.
perturbation.justification: The changed text directly contradicts the original contract by allowing the recipient to disclose confidential information based on their subjective assessment of 'public interest,' which undermines the confidentiality obligations. The Law Explanation indicates that this could potentially violate the Economic Espionage Act. Scraped Law Snippet 1 references the Economic Espionage Act of 1996, under which misappropriating trade secrets is a federal crime. The modified text's allowance of disclosure, even under a vague 'public interest' standard, creates a circumstance where the trade secrets could be misappropriated, conflicting with the original contract and potentially violating federal law.

perturbation.original_text: MS is not obligated to provide maintenance or updates to Recipient for the Product. However, any maintenance or updates provided by MS shall be covered by this Agreement.
perturbation.changed_text: MS may provide maintenance or updates to Recipient at its sole discretion. Any maintenance or updates are explicitly excluded from the terms of this agreement.
perturbation.explanation: This change creates a contradiction regarding the applicability of the agreement to provided maintenance and updates. The original text states any provided maintenance falls under the agreement while the modified text excludes such maintenance.
perturbation.contradicted_law: Uniform Commercial Code - Implied Warranty of Merchantability
perturbation.law_explanation: While this is a pre-release product, the UCC's implied warranty of merchantability (which normally applies to goods sold in commerce) suggests a basic level of fitness for the intended purpose. By providing updates and then explicitly excluding them from the agreement, it creates ambiguity. If MS provides an update that damages the recipient's system, excluding the update from the agreement could be interpreted as an attempt to disclaim responsibility for a product that isn't fit for purpose.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_1: § 2-314. Implied Warranty: Merchantability; Usage of Trade. | Uniform Commercial Code | US Law | LII / Legal Information Institute Please help us improve our site! × No thank you § 2-314. Implied Warranty: Merchantability; Usage of Trade. (1) Unless excluded or modified (Section 2-316 ), a warranty that the goods shall be merchantable is implied in a contract for their sale if the seller is a merchant with respect to goods of that kind. Under this section the serving for value of food or drink to be consumed either on the premises or elsewhere is a sale. (2) Goods to be merchantable must be at least such as (a) pass without objection in the trade under the contract description; and (b) in the case of fungible goods , are of fair average quality within the description; and (c) are fit for the ordinary purposes for which such goods are used; and (d) run, within the variations permitted by the agreement , of even kind, quality and quantity within each unit and among all units involved; and (e) are adequately contained, packaged, and labeled as the agreement may require; and (f) conform to the promise or affirmations of fact made on the container or label if any. (3) Unless excluded or modified (Section 2-316 ) other implied warranties may arise from course of dealing or usage of trade. Uniform Commercial Code Toolbox About Uniform Laws State Uniform Commercial Codes
perturbation.scraped_snippet_2: rship that put all the component commercial laws together in a comprehensive Uniform Commercial Code that was offered to the states for their consideration in 1951. Pennsylvania became the first state to adopt the UCC in 1953, and every other state followed suit over the next twenty years. News ULC Statement on Ownership of Investment Property under Uniform Commercial Code Article 8 ULC Statement on Central Bank Digital Currencies and the Uniform Commercial Code Cooperation with ALI The UCC is a joint project of the Uniform Law Commission and the American Law Institute. Recognizing that drafting a combined commercial code was a massive undertaking, the ULC invited ALI to participate in the codification project, and the ALI board accepted the invitation in 1942. Over the next ten years the two organizations collaborated at drafting meetings funded in large part by a generous grant from the Maurice and Laura Falk Foundation, with additional funding contributed from law firms, banks, and businesses that recognized the need for uniform commercial laws. The UCC Today The UCC is maintained under the guidance of the Permanent Editorial Board for the Uniform Commercial Code (PEB) , comprised of members appointed by the ULC and the ALI. The PEB, established in 1961, monitors developments in commercial law, recommends UCC amendments and revisions when necessary, and publishes official commentary to help courts interpret specific UCC provisions. An endowment established with the original Falk Foundation grant funding and replenished with UCC publishing royalties is available to fund UCC drafting projects. Articles Article 1, General Provisions Uniform Commercial Code Article 1 contains definitions and general provisions applicable as default rules to transactions covered under other articles of the UCC. Article 1 was last revised in 2001, with a few minor amendments since then to harmonize with recent revisions of other UCC articles. View Article 1, General Provisions Article 2, Sales Uniform Commercial Code Article 2 governs the sale of goods. It was part of the original Uniform Commercial Code approved in 1951. Article 2 represented a revision and modernization of the Uniform Sales Act, which was originally approved by the National Conference of Commissioners on Uniform State Laws in 1906. The Uniform Law Commission and American Law Institute approved a revised Article 2 in 2003 that was not adopted in any state, and was subsequently withdrawn by both organizations in 2011. Thus the 1951 version of Article 2 is the most recent official version. View Article 2, Sales Article 2A, Leases Uniform Commercial Code Article 2A governs leases of personal property. It was first added to the Uniform Commercial Code in 1987 and amended in 1990. A revision was approved by the Uniform Law Commission and the American Law Institute in 2003, but was not adopted in any jurisdiction and subsequently withdrawn by both organizations in 2011. Thus, the 1987 version of Article 2A, as amended in 1990, remains the official text. View Article 2A, Leases Article 3, Negotiable Instruments Uniform Commercial Code Article 3 governs negotiable instruments: drafts (including checks) and notes representing a promise to pay a sum of money, and that have independent value because they are negotiable. An instrument is negotiable if it can be transferred to another person and remain enforceable against the person who originally made the promise to pay. The substance of Article 3 has its roots in the Negotiable Instrument Law first approved by the National Conference of Commissioners on Uniform State Laws in 1896. That early uniform law was revised and incorporated into the original version of the UCC in 1951, and a further revision was approved in 1990. Finally, a set of amendments to UCC Articles 3 and 4 was approved in 2002. perturbation.justification: The modified text contradicts the original agreement regarding the applicability of its terms to maintenance and updates. The original text states that any maintenance or updates provided by MS shall be covered by the agreement. The changed text explicitly excludes such maintenance and updates. The Law Explanation states that excluding updates from the agreement could be interpreted as an attempt to disclaim responsibility for a product that isn't fit for purpose, potentially conflicting with the UCC's implied warranty of merchantability. Scraped Law Snippet 1 mentions that 'Unless excluded or modified (Section 2-316 ), a warranty that the goods shall be merchantable is implied in a contract for their sale if the seller is a merchant with respect to goods of that kind.' The modification shifts the responsibility and applicability of the agreement, creating ambiguity about liability, and directly alters the original terms, thus meeting the Misaligned Terminology - Legal Contradiction definition. This clear alteration and potential weakening of the UCC's implied warranty justify the 'YES' assessment.
